claim_information.color_of_animal: Chestnut with white blaze and two white hind socks
claim_information.tattoo_or_brand: Freeze brand on left shoulder: CRB-914

necropsy_details.names_of_all_participating_veterinarians: Laura K. Bennett, DVM; Samuel R. Ortiz, DVM
necropsy_details.history_and_symptoms:
Copper Ridge Blaze ("Blaze") was a 9-year-old chestnut Quarter Horse gelding used primarily for local hunter/jumper competitions and trail riding. According to the owner, Mr. Michael Harrington, Blaze had been in overall good health with routine preventive care, including vaccinations and deworming, and was last examined for an annual wellness check approximately 4 months prior to death, at which time no significant abnormalities were noted aside from mild hock stiffness consistent with age and activity level.

Two days prior to death (2025-04-15), the owner noticed Blaze was slightly off his feed at the evening meal, consuming approximately half his usual ration of a commercial performance pelleted feed and leaving most of his hay. He was described as slightly quieter than usual but still interactive and bright. The barn staff noted that he passed a normal amount of manure overnight, with normal consistency.

On the morning of 2025-04-16, Blaze was found in his stall sweating lightly, intermittently pawing at the ground, and looking at his flank. He had not finished his hay from the previous night, and there was less manure than normal in the stall (approximately 3 small piles instead of his usual 6–7). The barn manager took Blaze out of the stall and walked him for 20–30 minutes, noting that his discomfort seemed to wax and wane. The primary care veterinarian, Dr. Laura Bennett, was called to evaluate him on the farm.

On initial examination (approximately 09:15 on 2025-04-16), Blaze’s heart rate was recorded at 52 bpm (elevated), respiratory rate at 24 breaths per minute, and rectal temperature at 100.4°F (within normal limits). Mucous membranes were pink but slightly tacky, with a capillary refill time of approximately 2.5 seconds. Gut sounds were decreased in all quadrants, more notably on the right side. Nasogastric intubation yielded approximately 4 liters of reflux with sour odor but no obvious feed material. Rectal palpation revealed mild gas distention in the large colon but no obvious displacement or impaction at that time. He was treated with IV flunixin meglumine (Banamine), 10 mg/kg, and 10 L of IV polyionic fluids were started. He appeared more comfortable within 30 minutes, with reduced pawing and decreased flank-watching behavior.

Over the next 6–8 hours, Blaze’s signs of colic recurred intermittently, with periods of relative comfort alternating with episodes of sweating, pawing, stretching out as if to urinate, and occasional attempts to lie down and roll. Additional nasogastric intubation later in the afternoon produced approximately 6 liters of reflux. A second dose of flunixin was administered. Due to the recurrent nature of the signs and increasing concern for a more serious colic (e.g., displacement or strangulating lesion), Dr. Bennett recommended referral to an equine surgical facility.

Blaze was transported to Blue Valley Equine Hospital on the evening of 2025-04-16, arriving at approximately 19:30. On admission, his heart rate was 68 bpm, respiratory rate 28 breaths per minute, and he was moderately dehydrated (estimated 8% dehydration) with dark pink mucous membranes and a capillary refill time of 3 seconds. Abdominal ultrasound revealed distended small intestinal loops with decreased motility. Abdominocentesis yielded serosanguinous peritoneal fluid with elevated total protein and increased lactate, consistent with compromised intestinal perfusion.

After discussion with the owner regarding prognosis, cost, and risks, an exploratory laparotomy was recommended. Due to the late hour and the need to stabilize Blaze prior to anesthesia, surgery was scheduled for early morning 2025-04-17. Blaze was maintained overnight on IV fluids, analgesia, and monitoring. During the night he became increasingly uncomfortable despite pain management, with persistent tachycardia (70–80 bpm) and repeated attempts to roll.

On the morning of 2025-04-17, prior to surgery, Blaze experienced an acute deterioration, including sudden collapse in the stall, marked abdominal distension, and worsening cardiovascular parameters (weak pulse, cold extremities). Despite aggressive resuscitative efforts, including bolus IV fluids and emergency medications, his condition remained grave. Given the very poor prognosis for survival and likely extensive intestinal compromise, the owner elected humane euthanasia. Blaze was euthanized via intravenous barbiturate overdose on 2025-04-17 at approximately 08:10. The carcass was refrigerated and necropsy was performed the following morning (2025-04-18).
necropsy_details.findings:
EXTERNAL EXAMINATION:
The body is that of a 9-year-old chestnut Quarter Horse gelding in good to slightly overweight body condition (body condition score approximately 6/9). The coat is well-groomed with no evidence of ectoparasites. There is a distinct white blaze on the face and two white hind socks, consistent with the owner’s description. The freeze brand "CRB-914" is present on the left shoulder and is clearly legible. There is mild postmortem lividity on the dependent surfaces and early postmortem rigidity. No external traumatic injuries, lacerations, or contusions are noted.

HEAD AND NECK:
Oral cavity: Teeth show moderate wear consistent with age; no significant dental abnormalities visible externally. Mucous membranes are pale pink with mild postmortem drying.
Eyes: Mild corneal clouding consistent with postmortem changes. No evidence of trauma.
Neck: Jugular veins are intact with no evidence of thrombosis. No subcutaneous edema or hemorrhage observed.

THORACIC CAVITY:
Upon opening the thoracic cavity, there is no significant accumulation of fluid. The lungs are mildly congested and edematous, consistent with terminal hypoxia and euthanasia. No focal pneumonias or abscesses are observed. The trachea contains a small amount of frothy fluid. The heart is of normal size and shape. On sectioning, the myocardium appears grossly normal with no visible infarcts, scars, or areas of discoloration. The cardiac valves are thin and pliable, with no vegetations or gross abnormalities. The pericardial sac is intact and contains a small amount of clear fluid.

ABDOMINAL CAVITY:
Upon entering the abdominal cavity, a moderate amount of serosanguinous fluid is present, with strands of fibrin loosely adherent to the serosal surfaces of the small intestine and adjacent mesentery. The peritoneal surfaces of the small intestine are dull and reddened in multiple segments.

The small intestine (jejunum and ileum) is markedly distended over a length of approximately 10–12 meters, with gas and fluid. Several loops of small intestine in the mid-abdominal region exhibit dark red to purple discoloration, thickened walls, and loss of normal sheen, consistent with severe congestion and early necrosis. Mesenteric vessels supplying these segments are congested and contain several small thrombi.

A 360-degree volvulus (twist) of the mid-jejunum around the mesenteric root is identified, resulting in mechanical obstruction and compromised blood supply to a substantial portion of the small intestine. There is clear demarcation between the compromised, darkened segments and more proximal and distal segments that appear less affected but still distended. The mesentery adjacent to the volvulus is edematous with scattered petechial and ecchymotic hemorrhages.

The stomach is moderately distended with feed material and gas. There is mild reddening of the gastric mucosa, but no evidence of gastric rupture. The large colon and cecum are moderately gas-distended but maintain normal position; there is no evidence of large colon displacement, torsion, or impaction. The mucosa of the large colon is mildly congested but without ulceration or severe lesions.

LIVER, SPLEEN, AND OTHER ORGANS:
The liver is of normal size and shape with mild diffuse congestion. The capsular surface is smooth. On cut section, the parenchyma is homogeneous with no focal lesions. The spleen is mildly enlarged and congested, consistent with terminal circulatory changes. The kidneys are of normal size and shape, with corticomedullary distinction preserved. No gross calculi or lesions are evident in the kidneys or urinary bladder.

The pancreas, adrenal glands, and reproductive remnants (gelding) appear grossly unremarkable. There is no evidence of neoplasia or chronic organ disease on gross examination.

MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM:
No fractures, dislocations, or significant joint effusions are detected. Mild degenerative changes are present in the hock joints, including slight thickening of joint capsules and mild irregularity of articular cartilage surfaces, consistent with chronic, low-grade osteoarthritis expected for a 9-year-old performance horse.

HISTOPATHOLOGY (SELECTED TISSUES):
Representative samples of small intestine (affected and adjacent normal-appearing segments), large colon, liver, kidney, and heart were submitted for histopathologic examination. Microscopic evaluation of the affected small intestinal segments revealed severe transmural hemorrhage, coagulative necrosis of the mucosa and submucosa, and marked vascular congestion and thrombosis, confirming acute ischemic necrosis secondary to strangulating obstruction. Adjacent small intestinal segments showed milder mucosal edema and congestion. Liver sections demonstrated mild centrilobular congestion without significant hepatocellular degeneration. Kidney and heart samples were within normal limits.

SUMMARY OF NECROPSY FINDINGS:
The necropsy findings are most consistent with a strangulating small intestinal lesion caused by a 360-degree volvulus of the mid-jejunum, resulting in acute obstruction, severe vascular compromise, and ischemic necrosis of a substantial portion of the small intestine. Secondary changes include peritonitis, serosanguinous peritoneal effusion, and systemic circulatory compromise. No significant pre-existing systemic diseases were identified that would have independently contributed to death.
necropsy_details.cause_of_death:
Primary cause of death: Acute strangulating small intestinal obstruction due to 360-degree jejunal volvulus, resulting in severe vascular compromise, ischemic necrosis of affected intestinal segments, and subsequent cardiovascular collapse.

Manner of death: Humane euthanasia via intravenous barbiturate overdose following rapid clinical deterioration and grave prognosis associated with the small intestinal volvulus.

The necropsy findings, clinical history, and diagnostic workup are all consistent with an acute, surgically emergent colic due to a strangulating lesion of the small intestine. Given the extent of intestinal involvement observed at necropsy, the prognosis for survival, even with immediate surgical intervention, would have been very poor, with a high likelihood of postoperative complications, short-bowel syndrome, and guarded long-term outcome for performance use.
necropsy_details.tissues_explanation:
Yes. Representative tissue samples were collected and submitted to the diagnostic laboratory for histopathology and ancillary testing as follows:

1. Small Intestine (Affected Segment): Multiple sections from severely congested and discolored jejunal loops at the site of the volvulus were placed in 10% neutral buffered formalin for histopathologic confirmation of ischemic necrosis, evaluation of the extent of transmural involvement, and assessment of vascular changes (thrombosis, vasculitis).

2. Small Intestine (Adjacent, Less-Affected Segment): Sections from proximal and distal small intestine that appeared macroscopically less affected were also fixed for comparison, to determine the transition between viable and nonviable tissue and to document any underlying chronic enteropathy.

3. Large Colon and Cecum: Representative samples of large colon and cecal wall were collected to assess for concurrent colitis, mucosal ulceration, or chronic inflammatory changes that might not be evident grossly.

4. Liver: Samples from multiple lobes were submitted to evaluate for underlying hepatic disease, metabolic abnormalities, or changes secondary to shock and hypoxia (e.g., centrilobular congestion and degeneration).

5. Kidney: Cortex and medulla sections were collected to evaluate for acute tubular necrosis or other lesions related to hypoperfusion and systemic compromise.

6. Heart: Sections of left and right ventricular myocardium and interventricular septum were submitted to rule out underlying cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, or other primary cardiac lesions that could have contributed to circulatory failure.

7. Peritoneal Fluid: A sample of the serosanguinous peritoneal fluid was retained for cytologic examination and, if indicated, bacterial culture to further characterize the peritonitis.

Histopathology confirmed severe transmural ischemic necrosis of the affected small intestinal segments with vascular thrombosis and marked congestion, supporting the diagnosis of acute strangulating small intestinal obstruction due to volvulus. Other tissues showed only mild nonspecific changes consistent with shock and hypoxia, and no significant primary systemic disease was identified.
